dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 2 for chest pain at 15th and Main.
Greetings, I'm Aiden, an EMT with the rescue squad. What brought you to call 911 tonight?
I'm having severe chest pain that feels crushed and radiates to my back.
Sir, can you tell us your name?
Eyes are present, but we are focusing on responsiveness overall.
You're answering questions directly and appropriately.
Gripping and letting go normally. Motor response is within expected range.
You're keeping your airway open and clear on your own.
Respirations are 24 breaths a minute, and not labored at this time.
Pulse is 82. Skin is warm, dry, pale-and shows no critical circulatory abnormality.
When did the discomfort first start?
Started in the morning, but it's worse now in the evening.
Is there a part of your back that feels particularly significant?
Pain radiates to my back and feels like heavy pressure on my chest.
You mentioned nausea with the pain. Has it been continuous?
Yes, nausea has been with me since the pain started.
What prescriptions or over-the-counter medications are you using?
I'm on Xarelto, Cordarone, Norvasc, Microzide, Prinivil, and metformin.
Any significant heart events or diagnoses?
I have hyperlipidemia, hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and atrial fibrillation.
When did the pain notably increase?
Worse overall in the day and really spiked after 7 PM.
Does the discomfort vary with physical activity or rest?
Doesn't change. It's constant whether I move or sit still.
How would you set that chest sensation as described?
Like a crushing sensation that I can't shake, and I feel it in the back too.
Trigonum or other areas like arms or jaw? Just the back?
No, only the chest and spine specifically.
On a 10-point scale of pain, how severe would you say it is?
10-the worst I can imagine, honestly.
Has it worsened gradually or all at once recently?
Gradually the whole day and then it really spiked after sunset.
Skin is free of rashes, sores, or injuries on initial glance.
Pupils equal, round, and reactive. Neck is clear, no mass or jugular distension.
You're oriented and understand clearly what we're asking.
No signs of nasal obstruction or oral foreign body.
Chest wall is normal, breath sounds okay bilaterally.
I'm ready to attach you to the cardiac monitor now.
The EKG shows A-fib. Have you had this before, after the shocks?
The Cordarone I've been on seems to not help this episode at all.
Oxygen saturation is 95%. No signs of hypoxia immediately.
Initiate a 4-lead ECG, followed by a 12-lead as soon as possible.
Blood pressure measured as 128 over 92 per initial assessment data.
Respiratory rate remains 24, with no distress or accessory muscle use.
Pulse is 82. That still aligns with the A-fib presence.
Per first documented results, glucose is 130. No signs of low sugar.
Going in with the IV now. You're okay with this?
IV site is in the left arm via 20-gauge. Normal saline already running.
Giving nitroglycerin sublingually for chest pain-per standing protocol.
Following protocol 2-1 for suspected cardiac chest pain due to A-fib and symptoms.
Latest data from PCR, at 19:42, BP drops to 95 over 49.
Oxygen saturation rises to 99% with supplemental at 2L via nasal cannula.
Managing with fentanyl at 100 mcg per IV route to handle pain as recorded.
Those fentanyl doses continue as written with IV monitoring.
Pulse now at 92 as well for 19:42. Within safe thresholds.
That 200 mL saline bolus was completed between 19:36 and 19:48 per documentation.
Transporting to VCU Health-for their same medical records and continuing IV.
That's right. My care continuity needs to be there.
Don't move during transfer. Let's keep it smooth to the stretcher.
Your breathing seems stable, but be aware my support is just now in place.
Pain remains steady. Just nervous. That's understanding.
Setting you at semi-Fowler's for ease of breathing with IV in place.
All five-point restraints active now for patient safety en route.
Secondary restraints ready as well per transit policy each time.
Latest vitals at 19:52 show BP 116/81 and SpO2 at 97% as documented.
Patient reports ongoing back and chest discomfort and a stable rhythm.
Two fentanyl doses without reported improvement. Still complains of heaviness.
ER transfer by stretcher and five-point restraints per custom protocols and documentation.
The EKG shows a posterior wall infarct at V2 that handover later confirmed.
Monitor kept active during transport and handed directly at ER handover.
Per C-9 form and written notes, no meaningful change had happened in glucose or EKG readings.
All leads in place with restraints still fully working as noted in the PCB entry.
Final transfer completed to ER bed, as described in VCU ER log and PCR.
En route at semi-Fowler's with IV, not above, five-point restraints on board.
By PCR at 20:06, SpO2 was 98% with maintained, stable rhythm.
Final check at 20:06: pulse 71, BP 115/77, SpO2 at 98.
Administered additional fentanyl at 20:10. No缓解 as per PCR summary.
Any associated discomfort in the abdominal area such as cramping?
Nausea yes-but not cramps or gas. Just relating to the main pain.
Later at 19:52, the pulse was 88 with BP at 116 over 81 as tracked.
Patient had an existing A-fib rhythm-no cardiac arrest during our care as confirmed.
All supplies and documentation handed by ER nursing upon bed transfer and team briefing.
Patient positioned in semi-Fowler's for spotting stability from the rig to ER.
We should note that the patient is showing tachypnea-24 breaths per minute.
Additional measurements show consistent rates as per PCR with stable breathing.
